content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Participants                    Date : Jan. 27, 1982




From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Bob Leidigh
Subject: Review of Meeting Decisions of 1/21/82


     Please review the attached memo recapitulating the decisions
reached at our last meeting on several questions posed by Tony
Alberin. At our next meeting on Friday, January 29th, I would
like to receive confirmation of these decisions or have them re-
opened. Particularly as to question number C, I have gone into
some detail as to the legal (and practical) reasons supporting
our decision. On the other side of the coin, there are the
pragmatic considerations:

     (1) Our manual does not make it clear that pro-rata gross
income of a business is to be reported on Schedule D.

     (2) Our advice on this question appears to be inconsistent.

     (3) Tony Alberin will almost certainly request a formal
Commission Opinion; he claims that on 1/22/79 Barbara Campbell
gave him contrary advice.

     (4) If anyone owns 10% of a business entity it is also
likely that this interest is $1,000 or more in value, in which
case the investment in the business entity would be disclosed,
requiring disqualification, even though income might not be
disclosed if we were to require disclosure only of salary/
distributions. Given this reality, is there any real gain to
requiring disclosure of income?

Recapitulation of Decisions Reached at January 21, 1982, Opinion
Request Meeting.

Question:

A.   When a homeowner refinances his home through a commercial
     lending institution (loan over $10,000) is the loan report-
     able, or is it exempt from disclosure under Section 3203D(b)(8)(A)
     as a loan used to purchase the principal residence of the
     filer?

Opinion Request Participants
January 27, 1982
Page Two


Answer:  Section 82030(b)(A) and (B) reads as follows:

         (b)  "Income" also does not include:

              (8) Any loan or loans from a commercial
         lending institution which are made in the lender's
         regular course of business on terms available to
         members of the public without regard to official
         status if:

              (A)  Used to purchase the principal
         residence of filer; or

              (B)  The balance owed does not exceed
         ten thousand dollars ($10,000).

         Under a literal reading of this provision, any loan not
    actually used to purchase (acquire) the principal residence
    would be reportable if it exceeds $10,000.  However, times
    have changed and financing for homes now often includes
    short-term loans which must be refinanced or second trust
    deeds, etc.  It would seem that a second trust deed loan or
    a third trust deed loan taken out at the time of purchase
    would not be reportable while a refinancing loan would be
    reportable.

         In order to be equitable and to serve the legitimate
    purposes of the PRA, the Opinion Request Meeting participants
    concluded that the following rule should be followed:

         A loan, over $10,000 from a commercial lending institution,
    made in the lender's regular course of business on terms
    available to members of the public without regard to official
    status, is not reportable if:

         (a)  Used to purchase the principal residence of
    the filer, be it first, second, etc; or

         (b)  Secured by the principal residence of the
    filer and the proceeds of which are used exclusively to
    finance ownership or improvement of the principal residence
    of the filer and such usage is so limited by the terms
    of the loan agreement (i.e., the filer/borrower does
    not have discretionary use of the money).


Question:

B.    Tony Alperin's Questions (Variations on Theme)

      1.    Public official borrows dollars from commercial
            lending institution in excess of $10,000 and not

Opinion Request Participants
January 27, 1982
Page Three


                    for purchase of principal place of residence (i.e.,
                    reportable). Who is reported as source of loan
                    for 1982 Statement of Economic Interest in following
                    situations?

                    (a) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned
                        on 2/15/81 to Y. Both X and Y are commercial
                        lending institutions.

                    (b) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned
                        on 2/15/81 to Y; loan assigned 3/15/81 to Z.
                        X, Y and Z are all commercial lending institutions.

                    (c) Loan acquired 1/15/80 from X; loan assigned
                        1/15/81 to Y. Both X and Y are commercial
                        lending institutions.

                    (d) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned
                        2/15/81 to A. X is a commercial lending
                        institution, A is an individual.

    2.  Public official borrows dollars from individual
        who is not a relative (i.e., reportable). Who is
        reported as a source of loan for 1981 Statement of
        Economic Interest in following situations?

                    (a) Same as (a) in question 1 above, except X and
                        Y are individuals.

                    (b) Same as (b) in question 1 above, except X, Y
                        and Z are individuals.

                    (c) Same as (c) in question 1 above, except X and
                        Y are individuals.

Answer:

B.  1.     (a) Report both lending entities, indiciating
                that on assignment occurred.

            (b) Report all three lending entities, unless
                never owed payment to Y during the time Y had
                the loan.

            (c) Report Y only, unless made a payment to X
                during 1981. [illegible handwritten text]

            (d) Report both X and A. If loan were less than
                $10,000, then report only A.

Opinion Request Participants
January 27, 1982
Page Four


2.  (a) Report both individuals, indicating that an
assignment occurred.

(b) Report all three individuals, unless never
owed payment to Y.

(c) Report Y only, unless made a payment to X
during 1981.

Note: If individual assigns to a bank and loan balance
is less than $10,000 it would not be reportable at that
point if made on terms available to public in the regular
course of business. [illegible text]

Question:

B.  3.  Spouse of public official is guarantor on loan to
campaign. Is not called on to make good on guarantee.
Campaign reports this, but must public official
report anything on 721 form?

Answer: No.

Question:

C.  Public official and spouse jointly own business (leasing
real estate). If pro rata share of individual lessee's
gross payments exceeds $10,000, then report that lessee
as a source of income to business entity. What, if
anything is also reported in each of the following
instances:

1.  Business makes no profit. Public official and
spouse receive no disbursements (salary or otherwise)
Business has gross income in excess of $500.

2.  Business makes profit. It is left in bank, no
disbursements, no salary, etc. Profit exceeds
$500.

3.  Business makes profit. In excess of $500 distributed
to public official and spouse.

Answer:

C.  1.  Report pro rata share of gross income of business
entity (1/2).

2.  Same.

3.  Same.

Opinion Request Participants
January 27, 1982
Page Five


     This answer is based upon the following reasoning/analysis.

     Section 82030(a) defines the term "income" as used in
the PRA to include, inter alia, the following:

          Income of an individual also includes a pro rata
          share of any income of any business entity or
          trust in which the individual or spouse owns,
          directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent
          interest or greater.

     Section 87207 specifies required disclosure of income
and states, inter alia, as follows:

          (a)  When income is required to be reported
     under this article, the statement shall contain,
     except as provided in subsection (b):

               (1)  The name and address of each source
          of income aggregating ... ($250) or more in
          value ... and a general description of the
          business activity, if any, of each source;

               (2)  A statement whether the aggregate
          value of income from each source ... was at
          least ... ($250) but did not exceed ... ($1,000),
          whether it was in excess of ... ($1,000) but
          was not greater than ... ($10,000), or whether
          it was greater than ten thousand dollars
          ($10,000);

               (3)  A description of the consideration,
          if any, for which the income was received;

                         * * *

          (b)  When income of a business entity, including
     income of a sole proprietorship, is required to be
     reported under this article, the statement shall
     contain:

               (1)  The name, address and a general
          description of the business activity of the
          business entity;

               (2)  ...

Opinion Request Participants
January 27, 1982
Page Six


(3) In the case of a business entity
... the name of every person from whom the
business entity received payments if the
filer's pro rata share of gross receipts from
such person was equal to or greater than ten
thousand dollars ($10,000) during a calendar
year.

(Emphasis added.)

The underscored language in Section 87207 is important
to the interpretation of what is required to be reported.

Section 87207(a) speaks of "income" being reported. It
speaks in terms of "sources" of income. However, it also
contains the phrase "except as provided in subsection (b)."
Section (b), on the other hand, speaks in terms of "payments
received and "gross receipts" instead of "income."

Two possible interpretations can be drawn from these
semantics:

1. Since "income of an individual" includes "income of
a business entity" (Section 82030(a)), then 87207(a) can be
read to include the requirement that the pro rata share of
the business entity's income (gross) be reported under 87207(a).
Section 87207(b) would then be read to require additional
reporting of any customer/client where the pro rata share of
payments received is $10,000 or greater.

2. Since 87207(a) contains the phrase "except as provided
in subsection (b)," we look only to (b) to determine what is
required with regard to reporting of income of a business
entity.

We have determined to utilize the first interpretation.
The practical considerations involved are as follows: In
the case of a sole proprietorship where the public official
receives payments (income?) from clients/tenants in excess
of $250, but less than $10,000, there is no reporting require-
ment as to these individuals' identities. If there is no
profit/distribution/salary (very often the case in real
estate matters), then under analysis (2) above, there would
be no income reported. The interest in real estate would be
reported as such. Hopefully (?) it would also be reported
as an investment in a business entity. However, if it were
not, then there would be no indication that the reported
interest in real property was also serving as a business,
bringing in income. If it were not treated as a business
entity, then the public official would have to report, as

Opinion Request Participants
January 27, 1982
Page Seven


income, any receipts from any person equalling $250 or more
during the reporting period. By treating the rental property
as a business, the public official reaps the benefits of
only having to disclose individual lessees at the $10,000
level. He/she should not also reap the benefit of not disclosing gross income at all if there is a negative cash flow
or if the money is not distributed.

     In addition, in many other instances, businesses can
manipulate their bookkeeping so as to show no profit even
though they have substantial gross receipts. Should reporting of "income of a business entity" be dependent upon the
interval bookkeeping of that entity? The argument will be
made that our decision would require a 10% owner of General
Motors to report 10% of the General Motors' gross income.
This is largely falacious because, as has been shown in
several studies, no one owns 5%, let alone 10% of major
corporations such as General Motors.

REL:Km

sections.question:
A. When a homeowner refinances his home through a commercial lending institution (loan over $10,000) is the loan reportable, or is it exempt from disclosure under Section 82030(b)(8)(A) as a loan used to purchase the principal residence of the filer?

Answer: Section 82030(b)(A) and (B) reads as follows:

(b) "Income" also does not include:

(8) Any loan or loans from a commercial lending institution which are made in the lender's regular course of business on terms available to members of the public without regard to official status if:

(A) Used to purchase the principal residence of filer; or

(B) The balance owed does not exceed ten thousand dollars ($10,000).

Under a literal reading of this provision, any loan not actually used to purchase (acquire) the principal residence would be reportable if it exceeds $10,000. However, times have changed and financing for homes now often includes short-term loans which must be refinanced or second trust deeds, etc. It would seem that a second trust deed loan or a third trust deed loan taken out at the time of purchase would not be reportable while a refinancing loan would be reportable.

In order to be equitable and to serve the legitimate purposes of the PRA, the Opinion Request Meeting participants concluded that the following rule should be followed:

A loan, over $10,000 from a commercial lending institution, made in the lender's regular course of business on terms available to members of the public without regard to official status, is not reportable if:

(a) Used to purchase the principal residence of the filer, be it first, second, etc; or

(b) Secured by the principal residence of the filer and the proceeds of which are used exclusively to finance ownership or improvement of the principal residence of the filer and such usage is so limited by the terms of the loan agreement (i.e., the filer/borrower does not have discretionary use of the money).

Question:

B. Tony Alperin's Questions (Variations on Theme)

1. Public official borrows dollars from commercial lending institution in excess of $10,000 and not

for purchase of principal place of residence (i.e., reportable). Who is reported as source of loan for 1982 Statement of Economic Interest in following situations?

(a) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned on 2/15/81 to Y. Both X and Y are commercial lending institutions.

(b) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned on 2/15/81 to Y; loan assigned 3/15/81 to Z. X, Y and Z are all commercial lending institutions.

(c) Loan acquired 1/15/80 from X; loan assigned 1/15/81 to Y. Both X and Y are commercial lending institutions.

(d) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned 2/15/81 to A. X is a commercial lending institution, A is an individual.

2. Public official borrows dollars from individual who is not a relative (i.e., reportable). Who is reported as a source of loan for 1981 Statement of Economic Interest in following situations?

(a) Same as (a) in question 1 above, except X and Y are individuals.

(b) Same as (b) in question 1 above, except X, Y and Z are individuals.

(c) Same as (c) in question 1 above, except X and Y are individuals.

Answer:

B. 1. (a) Report both lending entities, indicating that on assignment occurred.

(b) Report all three lending entities, unless never owed payment to Y during the time Y had the loan.

(c) Report Y only, unless made a payment to X during 1981.

(d) Report both X and A. If loan were less than $10,000, then report only A.

2. (a) Report both individuals, indicating that an assignment occurred.

(b) Report all three individuals, unless never owed payment to Y.

(c) Report Y only, unless made a payment to X during 1981.

Note: If individual assigns to a bank and loan balance is less than $10,000 it would not be reportable at that point if made on terms available to public in the regular course of business.

Question:
B. 3. Spouse of public official is guarantor on loan to campaign. Is not called on to make good on guarantee. Campaign reports this, but must public official report anything on 721 form?

Answer: No.

Question:
C. Public official and spouse jointly own business (leasing real estate). If pro rata share of individual lessee's gross payments exceeds $10,000, then report that lessee as a source of income to business entity. What, if anything is also reported in each of the following instances:

1. Business makes no profit. Public official and spouse receive no disbursements (salary or otherwise) Business has gross income in excess of $500.

2. Business makes profit. It is left in bank, no disbursements, no salary, etc. Profit exceeds $500.

3. Business makes profit. In excess of $500 distributed to public official and spouse.

Answer:
C. 1. Report pro rata share of gross income of business entity (1/2).

2. Same.

3. Same.

This answer is based upon the following reasoning/analysis.

Section 82030(a) defines the term "income" as used in the PRA to include, inter alia, the following:

Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater.

Section 87207 specifies required disclosure of income and states, inter alia, as follows:

(a) When income is required to be reported under this article, the statement shall contain, except as provided in subsection (b):

(1) The name and address of each source of income aggregating ... ($250) or more in value ... and a general description of the business activity, if any, of each source;

(2) A statement whether the aggregate value of income from each source ... was at least ... ($250) but did not exceed ... ($1,000), whether it was in excess of ... ($1,000) but was not greater than ... ($10,000), or whether it was greater than ten thousand dollars ($10,000);

(3) A description of the consideration, if any, for which the income was received;
sections.conclusion:
A. A loan over $10,000 from a commercial lending institution is not reportable if: (a) Used to purchase the principal residence of the filer, be it first, second, etc; or (b) Secured by the principal residence of the filer and the proceeds are used exclusively to finance ownership or improvement of the principal residence.

B. 1. Report the original lender and current lender (or current lender only if payment made only to current lender); 2. Report the original lender and any individual assignees; 3. No reporting required if spouse's guarantee is not called.

C. Report pro rata share of gross income of business entity (1/2) in all instances, regardless of whether business makes profit or receives distributions.
sections.question_synthetic: The memorandum addresses three main questions: (1) whether refinance loans for principal residences over $10,000 are reportable; (2) how to identify the source of loans when they are assigned between multiple lenders; and (3) what income must be reported for jointly owned businesses with no profit distributions or negative cash flow.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that refinance loans secured by a principal residence qualify for the exemption if proceeds are used exclusively for ownership or improvement. When loans are assigned, the filer must report all lenders to whom payment was made. For jointly-owned businesses, the pro rata share of gross income must be reported regardless of profit distribution status.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document is an internal FPPC memo recapping meeting decisions rather than a formal advice letter response to a single requester. Contains three main Question/Answer sections with detailed legal analysis, particularly regarding Section 82030 and 87207 interpretation. Memo references internal concerns about consistency and potential formal opinion request.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A. When a homeowner refinances his home through a commercial lending institution (loan over $10,000) is the loan reportable, or is it exempt from disclosure under Section 82030(b)(8)(A) as a loan used to purchase the principal residence of the filer?

Answer: Section 82030(b)(A) and (B) reads as follows:

(b) "Income" also does not include:

(8) Any loan or loans from a commercial lending institution which are made in the lender's regular course of business on terms available to members of the public without regard to official status if:

(A) Used to purchase the principal residence of filer; or

(B) The balance owed does not exceed ten thousand dollars ($10,000).

Under a literal reading of this provision, any loan not actually used to purchase (acquire) the principal residence would be reportable if it exceeds $10,000. However, times have changed and financing for homes now often includes short-term loans which must be refinanced or second trust deeds, etc. It would seem that a second trust deed loan or a third trust deed loan taken out at the time of purchase would not be reportable while a refinancing loan would be reportable.

In order to be equitable and to serve the legitimate purposes of the PRA, the Opinion Request Meeting participants concluded that the following rule should be followed:

A loan, over $10,000 from a commercial lending institution, made in the lender's regular course of business on terms available to members of the public without regard to official status, is not reportable if:

(a) Used to purchase the principal residence of the filer, be it first, second, etc; or

(b) Secured by the principal residence of the filer and the proceeds of which are used exclusively to finance ownership or improvement of the principal residence of the filer and such usage is so limited by the terms of the loan agreement (i.e., the filer/borrower does not have discretionary use of the money).

Question:

B. Tony Alperin's Questions (Variations on Theme)

1. Public official borrows dollars from commercial lending institution in excess of $10,000 and not

for purchase of principal place of residence (i.e., reportable). Who is reported as source of loan for 1982 Statement of Economic Interest in following situations?

(a) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned on 2/15/81 to Y. Both X and Y are commercial lending institutions.

(b) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned on 2/15/81 to Y; loan assigned 3/15/81 to Z. X, Y and Z are all commercial lending institutions.

(c) Loan acquired 1/15/80 from X; loan assigned 1/15/81 to Y. Both X and Y are commercial lending institutions.

(d) Loan acquired 1/15/81 from X; loan assigned 2/15/81 to A. X is a commercial lending institution, A is an individual.

2. Public official borrows dollars from individual who is not a relative (i.e., reportable). Who is reported as a source of loan for 1981 Statement of Economic Interest in following situations?

(a) Same as (a) in question 1 above, except X and Y are individuals.

(b) Same as (b) in question 1 above, except X, Y and Z are individuals.

(c) Same as (c) in question 1 above, except X and Y are individuals.

Answer:

B. 1. (a) Report both lending entities, indicating that on assignment occurred.

(b) Report all three lending entities, unless never owed payment to Y during the time Y had the loan.

(c) Report Y only, unless made a payment to X during 1981.

(d) Report both X and A. If loan were less than $10,000, then report only A.

2. (a) Report both individuals, indicating that an assignment occurred.

(b) Report all three individuals, unless never owed payment to Y.

(c) Report Y only, unless made a payment to X during 1981.

Note: If individual assigns to a bank and loan balance is less than $10,000 it would not be reportable at that point if made on terms available to public in the regular course of business.

Question:
B. 3. Spouse of public official is guarantor on loan to campaign. Is not called on to make good on guarantee. Campaign reports this, but must public official report anything on 721 form?

Answer: No.

Question:
C. Public official and spouse jointly own business (leasing real estate). If pro rata share of individual lessee's gross payments exceeds $10,000, then report that lessee as a source of income to business entity. What, if anything is also reported in each of the following instances:

1. Business makes no profit. Public official and spouse receive no disbursements (salary or otherwise) Business has gross income in excess of $500.

2. Business makes profit. It is left in bank, no disbursements, no salary, etc. Profit exceeds $500.

3. Business makes profit. In excess of $500 distributed to public official and spouse.

Answer:
C. 1. Report pro rata share of gross income of business entity (1/2).

2. Same.

3. Same.

This answer is based upon the following reasoning/analysis.

Section 82030(a) defines the term "income" as used in the PRA to include, inter alia, the following:

Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater.

Section 87207 specifies required disclosure of income and states, inter alia, as follows:

(a) When income is required to be reported under this article, the statement shall contain, except as provided in subsection (b):

(1) The name and address of each source of income aggregating ... ($250) or more in value ... and a general description of the business activity, if any, of each source;

(2) A statement whether the aggregate value of income from each source ... was at least ... ($250) but did not exceed ... ($1,000), whether it was in excess of ... ($1,000) but was not greater than ... ($10,000), or whether it was greater than ten thousand dollars ($10,000);

(3) A description of the consideration, if any, for which the income was received;

CONCLUSION: A. A loan over $10,000 from a commercial lending institution is not reportable if: (a) Used to purchase the principal residence of the filer, be it first, second, etc; or (b) Secured by the principal residence of the filer and the proceeds are used exclusively to finance ownership or improvement of the principal residence.

B. 1. Report the original lender and current lender (or current lender only if payment made only to current lender); 2. Report the original lender and any individual assignees; 3. No reporting required if spouse's guarantee is not called.

C. Report pro rata share of gross income of business entity (1/2) in all instances, regardless of whether business makes profit or receives distributions.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC memorandum recapitulating decisions from a January 21, 1982 Opinion Request Meeting regarding reportable loans, loan assignments, and business income disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act. The memo addresses questions from Tony Alperin about refinancing exemptions, loan assignment reporting, and pro rata income reporting for jointly-owned businesses.
